bibliographic.citation: Robert Kilburn Root, 'Poems from the Garrett MS,' Englische Studien 41 (1910): 360-379.
bibliographic.title: Poems from the Garrett MS

entities.description: The immediate print source. Root introduces Garrett MS 143 and its contents on pp. 360-361, compares the four poems with other witnesses on pp. 361-364, and prints the Garrett texts on pp. 365-379. CME00084 encodes the four printed texts, not the complete article.
entities.label: Robert Kilburn Root, 'Poems from the Garrett MS' (1910)
notes: Root's article is signed at Princeton and identifies the manuscript as then deposited in Princeton University Library.

Root's folio-side notation uses a/b. The current Princeton surrogate uses recto/verso and places the first poem's rubric and opening on fol. 38v; Root's first in-text folio milestone occurs after line 19 at fol. 39a.

rights.component: 1910 article text, transcription, and editorial matter

access.accessMethod: Read the exact repository record and contents list.

dateStatements.label: Production
dateStatements.value: Around the beginning of the fifteenth century (Princeton display date 1400; Root: apparently first half of the fifteenth century)

entities.description: A small composite devotional miscellany containing prose and verse in Middle English and Latin. Root prints its final four texts, at current fols. 38v-51v.
entities.label: Princeton University Library, Garrett MS 143
notes: Princeton's contents list gives the exact target loci: Trental of St Gregory, ff. 38v-44v; Saying the Best, ff. 44v-46v; Thank God of All, ff. 47r-49r; Making Amends, ff. 49r-51v.
The four poems are the codex's final contents; the complete physical witness also includes eight earlier devotional prose items on ff. 1r-38r.

physicalDescription.dimensions: 140 x 95 mm (current catalog); Root measured the written volume at 97 x 131 mm and the ruled space at 59 x 89 mm
physicalDescription.extent: 51 leaves, with three front and three rear flyleaves represented in the digital surrogate
notes: Princeton identifies the script as textualis semi-quadrata and describes blue two- to three-line initials with red flourishing.
The manuscript has 20 lines per page according to Root.
The nineteenth-century English binding by Francis Bedford is brown morocco over pasteboards; its spine title is 'A treatise on the ten commandments. MS.'

rights.basis: The texts are attested in medieval manuscripts, including Garrett MS 143 around the beginning of the fifteenth century.
notes: This does not determine rights or contractual terms for Root's editorial matter, the U-M encoding, catalog descriptions, manuscript photographs, or interface assets.

localCopies.label: Complete 118-canvas Princeton facsimile of Garrett MS 143 at 1800-pixel width
notes: Complete for every provider-exposed surface: front and back covers, pastedowns, six front and six rear flyleaf surfaces, and fols. 1r-51v. Princeton exposes no separate spine or edge views.
All 118 images were retrieved with effective IIIF width requests of 1800,; the exact provider manifest and per-image checksums and URLs are retained in the bundle.

Visual inspection confirms a full-color sequence preserving binding color, parchment tone, red rubrics and flourishing, and blue initials, notwithstanding the catalog's 'Digitized microfilm copy' label.

workPortion.label: The four poems in CME00084 within complete Garrett MS 143
notes: Poem I: fols. 38v-44v, canvases 84-96; poem II: fols. 44v-46v, canvases 96-100; poem III: fols. 47r-49r, canvases 101-105; poem IV: fols. 49r-51v, canvases 105-110.
The first and second poems share fol. 44v / canvas 96; the third and fourth share fol. 49r / canvas 105.

entities.description: The version 1.0 Digital Index of Middle English Verse records that establish four separate intellectual works, identify the Garrett loci and Root page ranges, and enumerate their wider manuscript and edition traditions.
entities.label: DIMEV records for the four poems in CME00084
notes: Record 2777 lists six witnesses of The Trental of St Gregory; record 5311 lists two witnesses of All Way Fond to Say the Best; record 925 lists seven witnesses of Thank God of All; and record 926 lists four witnesses of For Thy Sins Amends Make.
The collateral witnesses documented by DIMEV belong to the wider traditions. They are not modeled as sources of Root's Garrett transcription.

rights.basis: The versioned release includes a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 license.

reviewNotes: The cataloging subject is Root's four-poem printed selection, not one medieval work, the complete article, or the complete manuscript.
The whole manuscript was cached before mapping the work portion; shared boundary surfaces at fols. 44v and 49r are included in both adjacent poem mappings.
Root's a/b folio notation, Princeton's current recto/verso labels, and PDF/IIIF positions are retained as distinct locator systems.
DIMEV's wider witness census is recorded as scholarly context without retroactively making collateral witnesses sources of Root's transcription.

workPortion.label: Four DIMEV records associated with CME00084

sourceLinks.provider: Digital Index of Middle English Verse

rights.basis: The versioned release includes a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 license.

lineage.summary: CME00084 is the repository copy of a University of Michigan encoding of the four poem texts on Robert Kilburn Root's printed pp. 365-379, selected from his article 'Poems from the Garrett MS' in Englische Studien 41 (1910), pp. 360-379. Root says the text is, apart from punctuation, an exact transcription of Princeton University Library, Garrett MS 143; he leaves scribal errors uncorrected, expands contractions in italics, and supplies stanza spacing for the final three lyrics. The manuscript is a 51-leaf devotional miscellany made in England around the beginning of the fifteenth century; the four poems occupy current fols. 38v-51v. Princeton now exposes a complete 118-canvas full-color sequence containing covers, pastedowns, twelve flyleaf surfaces, and every manuscript leaf. The whole sequence and Princeton's generated 119-page PDF are cached locally, with the four-poem portion mapped to IIIF canvases 84-110 and PDF pages 85-111. A complete 480-page scan of Englische Studien 41 and the complete DIMEV 1.0 release are also cached. DIMEV identifies the four intellectual works as records 2777, 5311, 925, and 926 (IMEV 1653, 3371, 562, and 563) and documents their wider witness traditions without making those collateral copies sources of Root's text.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous authors (four poems; common authorship is not asserted)
catalogSummary.date: Constituent composition dates unresolved; the source witness was made around the beginning of the fifteenth century; Root's exact selection was first published in 1910
catalogSummary.region: Places of composition unresolved; the source witness was made in England, which is not transferred to all four poems' composition
catalogSummary.title: Poems from the Garrett MS

catalogingSubject.label: Poems from the Garrett MS
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the exact four-poem Middle English selection encoded in CME00084 from Robert Kilburn Root's 1910 article: the poem texts on printed pp. 365-379, without Root's preceding manuscript description and comparative discussion on pp. 360-364. The four anonymous poems are separate intellectual works copied consecutively at the end of Garrett MS 143; the unit is not one medieval work, the complete article, or the complete manuscript.

contentParts.label: I. The Trental of St Gregory
notes: DIMEV record 2777; IMEV/NIMEV 1653.
The four XML LG containers are representation groups, not four poem stanzas; DIMEV identifies the poem's verse form as rhymed couplets (aa).
contentParts.summary: An exemplum in which Pope Gregory's mother hides sexual sin and infanticide, dies unconfessed, appears to her son from purgatorial torment, and is released through a prescribed trental of masses and prayer; an appended instruction explains how to perform the observance.

contentParts.label: II. All Way Fond to Say the Best
notes: DIMEV record 5311; IMEV/NIMEV 3371; ten eight-line ababbcbc stanzas.
Fol. 44v / canvas 96 also contains the end of the preceding Trental.
contentParts.summary: A ten-stanza didactic lyric counseling restraint of speech: avoid evil tales, slander, defamation, and repetition of others' faults, and seek God's and society's favor by saying the best.

contentParts.label: III. Thank God of All
notes: DIMEV record 925; IMEV/NIMEV 562; eleven eight-line ababbcbc stanzas in Garrett, while the number and order vary across the broader tradition.
Fol. 49r / canvas 105 also contains the opening of the following Making Amends lyric.
contentParts.summary: An eleven-stanza alliterative lyric teaching gratitude to God through sickness, poverty, imprisonment, bereavement, and social inequality, invoking Job as an example and repeating the counsel to thank God of all.

contentParts.label: IV. For Thy Sins Amends Make
notes: DIMEV record 926; IMEV/NIMEV 563; twelve eight-line ababbcbc stanzas.
The first eight lines precede Root's fol. 49b milestone because the poem begins on current fol. 49r, a shared surface with the preceding lyric.
contentParts.summary: A twelve-stanza memento mori and penitential lyric in which a solitary walker hears birds teach that death is inevitable, worldly rank cannot prevent it, and confession, repentance, charity, and making amends must not be delayed.

assertion.notes: This is the first publication date of Root's exact four-poem selection, not necessarily the first publication of every constituent poem and not a medieval composition date.

extent.description: Four DIV1 poem units with 522 L elements in 37 LG elements. The last three poems account for 33 genuine eight-line stanza groups (10, 11, and 12); the first poem's four LG wrappers divide 258 rhymed-couplet lines but do not represent a four-stanza intellectual structure.

formStatements.description: All four constituent works are verse. The XML has 522 L elements in 37 LG elements; the first poem is in rhymed couplets, while the final three have 10, 11, and 12 eight-line stanzas respectively.

assertion.notes: Root's modern English introduction and notes are edition-layer matter outside the cataloged poem selection and are not added as work-level languages.

notes: The preferred title is Root's established edition title, while the unit type and scope note prevent it from being mistaken for a medieval collection title or for Root's complete article.
No composition place is asserted: Princeton's England statement dates and localizes the source witness, not necessarily all four poems' creation.
Root's modern English introduction and comparative notes are edition-layer context outside the cataloged selection and therefore do not make the work-level language facet bilingual.
The complete Garrett MS 143 facsimile is cached first; the selection is then mapped to fols. 38v-51v / canvases 84-110, including shared boundary surfaces at fols. 44v and 49r.
The first poem's italic passages in CME00084 are Root's expansion typography; consult the manuscript images for the original contraction forms.

next_steps: Consult current poem-specific linguistic and textual studies for IMEV 1653, 3371, 562, and 563.
Keep composition separate from Garrett MS 143's production in England around the beginning of the fifteenth century.
Do not assign one author, composition date, dialect region, or composition place to Root's artificial four-poem selection without part-level evidence.
openQuestions.question: When and where were the four constituent poems composed, and how do the Garrett versions relate chronologically to their variant traditions?

next_steps: Retain DIMEV's record-level titles for discovery and the visible Garrett rubrics as alternates.
Consult current critical editions before treating refrain titles as titles shared identically by every version.
Preserve Garrett-specific stanza counts and loci in part notes.
openQuestions.question: Which modern preferred titles best distinguish each Garrett version from substantially different versions in the wider manuscript tradition?

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous poets of the four selected poems

scope.description: Anonymous authorship of the four medieval poems, without a claim of common authorship

assertion.notes: Root explicitly describes the printed text as an exact transcript apart from punctuation.

scope.description: Transcription of the four Garrett MS 143 poem texts

subjects.label: Purgatory, masses, and prayer for the dead
Secret sin, confession, penance, and salvation
Restraint of speech, slander, and reputation
Job as an example of patience

summaries.value: Robert Kilburn Root's 1910 selection prints the four anonymous Middle English verse works that conclude Princeton Garrett MS 143. The first narrates Saint Gregory's trental and the release of his mother from purgatory; the following refrain lyrics counsel charitable speech, gratitude to God in adversity, and penitential amendment before death. Root's edition preserves scribal errors, adds punctuation, expands contractions in italics, and supplies stanza spacing for the final three lyrics.

Four numbered poem units on printed pp. 365-379 contain 522 encoded verse lines: The Trental of St Gregory, 258 lines; All Way Fond to Say the Best, 80 lines in ten stanzas; Thank God of All, 88 lines in eleven stanzas; and For Thy Sins Amends Make, 96 lines in twelve stanzas. They occupy current manuscript fols. 38v-51v and Princeton IIIF canvases 84-110.

assertion.notes: Root's established article title is retained for the encoded four-poem selection; the scope note distinguishes it from the complete article.
A manuscript rubric visible on current fol. 38v, not merely a supplied catalog heading.
DIMEV's discovery title is taken from the refrain; the witness rubric calls it the song of saying the best.

titles.value: All Way Fond to Say the Best
Here begynneþ þe songe of saing þe best
Here begynneþ þe songe of þonkynge god of al

assertion.notes: A normalized form of DIMEV's refrain title 'For þi Sunnes Amendes make.'

titles.value: Here begynne þe songe of amendis makinge

work.summary: Robert Kilburn Root's 1910 selection prints the four anonymous Middle English verse works that conclude Princeton Garrett MS 143. The first narrates Saint Gregory's trental and the release of his mother from purgatory; the following refrain lyrics counsel charitable speech, gratitude to God in adversity, and penitential amendment before death. Root's edition preserves scribal errors, adds punctuation, expands contractions in italics, and supplies stanza spacing for the final three lyrics.
